content.text: Refactoring Discipline for GenAI Integration Code

GenAI integration code is unusually prone to accruing technical debt quickly. The first working version is almost always a single function that queries the database, builds the prompt, calls the LLM, and logs the result — all in one place. This works for a demo, but it is nearly impossible to test, costly to change safely, and opaque when production incidents occur.
Refactoring this code requires more discipline than ordinary application refactoring because the correctness signal is probabilistic: a prompt refactor that
equivalent in unit tests may still cause a 10% drop in eval scores on edge-case inputs. This sub-subtopic covers the patterns and safeguards that make GenAI integration refactoring safe.

attrs.alt: GenAI integration code: before and after refactoring into separated layers

content.text: The Four Layers of a GenAI Integration
The canonical refactoring target for GenAI integration code is a four-layer architecture where each layer has a single responsibility and can be tested independently of the others:
Assemble the prompt string from structured data

composes these layers in sequence and contains no logic of its own — it is a thin coordinator whose only job is calling the layers in order and propagating errors. Any business logic that ends up in the orchestrator is a sign that a new layer is needed.

The prompt layer is the highest-value extraction target because it is the artefact most likely to change over the lifetime of a GenAI feature. Keeping it in a pure function — one that takes structured data and returns a string with no I/O side effects — makes it independently version-controlled, snapshot-testable, and reviewable by the prompt engineering team without touching application code.

# Before: prompt assembled inline in the handler
async def handle_query(query: str, user_id: str) -> str:
    user = await db.get_user(user_id)
    prompt = f"You are a customer support assistant. The user's name is {user.name}. "
    prompt += f"Their account tier is {user.tier}. They ask: {query}"
    resp = await llm_client.complete(prompt)
    return resp


# After: pure prompt builder extracted to its own module
# prompts/user_query.py
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class UserContext:
    name: str
    tier: str       # "free" | "pro" | "enterprise"
    locale: str

def build_user_query_prompt(ctx: UserContext, query: str) -> str:
    """
    Returns the assembled user query prompt.
    Pure function — no I/O, no side effects.
    """
    tier_guidance = {
        "free":       "Offer helpful answers; upsell Pro features where relevant.",
        "pro":        "Provide detailed technical answers without upsell messaging.",
        "enterprise": "Prioritise SLA compliance and escalation paths.",
    }.get(ctx.tier, "Provide a helpful answer.")

    return (
        f"You are a customer support assistant for AcmeCorp.\n"
        f"User: {ctx.name} (locale: {ctx.locale})\n"
        f"Account tier: {ctx.tier}\n"
        f"Guidance: {tier_guidance}\n\n"
        f"User question: {query}"
    )

# tests/prompts/test_user_query.py
import pytest
from prompts.user_query import UserContext, build_user_query_prompt

def test_pro_tier_excludes_upsell():
    ctx = UserContext(name="Alice", tier="pro", locale="en-US")
    prompt = build_user_query_prompt(ctx, "How do I export data?")
    assert "upsell" not in prompt.lower()
    assert "Pro features" not in prompt

def test_enterprise_tier_includes_sla():
    ctx = UserContext(name="Bob", tier="enterprise", locale="en-GB")
    prompt = build_user_query_prompt(ctx, "Where is the SLA document?")
    assert "SLA" in prompt or "escalation" in prompt.lower()

def test_prompt_snapshot(snapshot):
    ctx = UserContext(name="Carol", tier="free", locale="en-US")
    prompt = build_user_query_prompt(ctx, "What is your refund policy?")
    snapshot.assert_match(prompt, "free_tier_refund_prompt.txt")

The LLM client layer wraps the API call and centralises all operational concerns: model selection, retry logic, timeout, token budget enforcement, and cost attribution. Every part of the codebase that needs to call an LLM goes through this gateway — none should import the SDK directly.

# llm/gateway.py
import asyncio, time
from dataclasses import dataclass
from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic, APIError, RateLimitError

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class LLMConfig:
    model: str = "claude-sonnet-5"
    max_tokens: int = 1024
    temperature: float = 1.0
    timeout_seconds: float = 30.0
    max_retries: int = 3

class LLMGateway:
    def __init__(self, client: AsyncAnthropic, config: LLMConfig):
        self._client = client
        self._config = config

    async def complete(self, prompt: str, *, trace_id: str | None = None) -> str:
        """
        Call the LLM with retry + timeout. Returns the completion text.
        Raises LLMGatewayError on exhausted retries.
        """
        last_err: Exception | None = None
        for attempt in range(self._config.max_retries):
            try:
                resp = await asyncio.wait_for(
                    self._client.messages.create(
                        model=self._config.model,
                        max_tokens=self._config.max_tokens,
                        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
                    ),
                    timeout=self._config.timeout_seconds,
                )
                return resp.content[0].text
            except RateLimitError as e:
                wait = 2 ** attempt
                await asyncio.sleep(wait)
                last_err = e
            except (APIError, asyncio.TimeoutError) as e:
                last_err = e
                if attempt < self._config.max_retries - 1:
                    await asyncio.sleep(2 ** attempt)
        raise LLMGatewayError(f"LLM call failed after {self._config.max_retries} attempts") from last_err


class LLMGatewayError(Exception):
    pass

# tests/llm/test_gateway.py
import pytest
from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, patch
from llm.gateway import LLMConfig, LLMGateway, LLMGatewayError
from anthropic import RateLimitError

@pytest.fixture
def mock_client():
    client = AsyncMock()
    client.messages.create.return_value.content = [AsyncMock(text="Test response")]
    return client

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_complete_returns_text(mock_client):
    gw = LLMGateway(mock_client, LLMConfig())
    result = await gw.complete("Hello")
    assert result == "Test response"

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_retries_on_rate_limit(mock_client):
    mock_client.messages.create.side_effect = [
        RateLimitError("rate limit", response=None, body={}),
        AsyncMock(content=[AsyncMock(text="Retried response")])(),
    ]
    gw = LLMGateway(mock_client, LLMConfig(max_retries=3))
    result = await gw.complete("Hello")
    assert result == "Retried response"
    assert mock_client.messages.create.call_count == 2

The critical difference between refactoring ordinary code and refactoring GenAI integration code is the correctness signal. For ordinary code, a test suite with high coverage gives strong confidence that a refactor is safe. For GenAI code, unit tests can pass while the refactor causes a measurable regression in model output quality — because the tests mock the LLM response and never exercise the prompt with a real model.

: run a curated set of representative inputs through the real LLM before and after the refactor and compare scores. The refactor is only safe to merge if eval scores are within an acceptable tolerance.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""eval_gate.py — compare prompt output quality before and after a refactor."""
import asyncio, json
from dataclasses import dataclass
from pathlib import Path
from llm.gateway import LLMGateway, LLMConfig
from prompts.user_query import UserContext, build_user_query_prompt
from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic

@dataclass
class EvalCase:
    id: str
    ctx: UserContext
    query: str
    expected_keywords: list[str]  # must appear in response
    forbidden_keywords: list[str]  # must NOT appear


def score_response(response: str, case: EvalCase) -> float:
    """Simple keyword presence score (0.0–1.0). Replace with LLM-judge for production."""
    hits = sum(1 for kw in case.expected_keywords if kw.lower() in response.lower())
    misses = sum(1 for kw in case.forbidden_keywords if kw.lower() in response.lower())
    total = len(case.expected_keywords) + len(case.forbidden_keywords)
    return (hits + (len(case.forbidden_keywords) - misses)) / total if total else 1.0


async def run_eval(cases: list[EvalCase], gateway: LLMGateway) -> dict[str, float]:
    scores = {}
    for case in cases:
        prompt = build_user_query_prompt(case.ctx, case.query)
        response = await gateway.complete(prompt)
        scores[case.id] = score_response(response, case)
    return scores


EVAL_CASES = [
    EvalCase(
        id="pro_no_upsell",
        ctx=UserContext(name="Dev", tier="pro", locale="en-US"),
        query="How do I use the API?",
        expected_keywords=["API", "documentation"],
        forbidden_keywords=["upgrade", "Pro features", "upsell"],
    ),
    EvalCase(
        id="free_refund",
        ctx=UserContext(name="Alice", tier="free", locale="en-US"),
        query="What is your refund policy?",
        expected_keywords=["refund", "policy"],
        forbidden_keywords=[],
    ),
]

TOLERANCE = 0.05  # allow ≤5% score drop post-refactor

async def main():
    gw = LLMGateway(AsyncAnthropic(), LLMConfig())
    print("Running eval suite...")
    scores = await run_eval(EVAL_CASES, gw)

    baseline_path = Path("evals/baseline_scores.json")
    if baseline_path.exists():
        baseline = json.loads(baseline_path.read_text())
        regressions = {k: (baseline[k], v) for k, v in scores.items()
                       if k in baseline and baseline[k] - v > TOLERANCE}
        if regressions:
            print(f"FAIL: {len(regressions)} eval regressions exceed {TOLERANCE*100:.0f}% tolerance:")
            for case_id, (before, after) in regressions.items():
                print(f"  {case_id}: {before:.2f} → {after:.2f} (drop: {before-after:.2f})")
            raise SystemExit(1)
        print(f"PASS: all {len(scores)} evals within tolerance")
    else:
        baseline_path.parent.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
        baseline_path.write_text(json.dumps(scores, indent=2))
        print(f"Baseline established: {scores}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())
Apply this checklist to every GenAI integration refactoring PR before requesting review:
Reviewable by prompt team without application context
Catches accidental prompt drift from future refactors
Centralised retry, timeout, and cost tracking
No raw strings passed to LLM
All prompts built from typed structured data — prevents injection
Unit tests passing is necessary but not sufficient
Enables latency and cost attribution in production
Model upgrades require config change, not code change
Reviewers can verify quality was not sacrificed for cleanliness

— changing the prompt text and the calling code in the same PR makes it impossible to attribute an eval regression to one or the other. Separate prompt changes from code structure changes into sequential PRs.
Mocking the LLM in eval tests
— unit tests must mock the LLM, but eval tests must not. If all tests use mocks, the only way to detect a prompt regression is through a production incident.
Hardcoding model versions in the prompt layer
— the model name belongs in configuration (injected via
call inside the prompt layer. This allows model upgrades without changing prompt code.
— if the orchestrator grows beyond 20 lines, it is accumulating logic that belongs in a layer. Treat orchestrator growth as a smell and extract immediately.
Skipping the eval gate for 'trivial' refactors

— renaming variables and extracting helper functions are trivially safe, but changing any string that flows into a prompt, any data that shapes the prompt, or any configuration that changes model behaviour requires an eval gate run.

Refactoring the integration code is also the right moment to add structured observability — emitting trace events that record prompt content, model response, latency, token counts, and eval scores per request. Without this layer, production AI behaviour is a black box: you know something went wrong but cannot replay the exact prompt and context that caused it.

# observability/trace.py
import time, uuid
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any
import structlog

log = structlog.get_logger()

@dataclass
class LLMTraceEvent:
    trace_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    span_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    service: str = "unknown"
    prompt_hash: str = ""          # SHA256 of prompt — never log prompt text in prod
    model: str = ""
    input_tokens: int = 0
    output_tokens: int = 0
    latency_ms: float = 0.0
    status: str = "ok"            # "ok" | "error" | "timeout" | "rate_limited"
    error_type: str | None = None
    eval_score: float | None = None
    metadata: dict[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)

    def emit(self) -> None:
        log.info("llm_trace",
            trace_id=self.trace_id,
            span_id=self.span_id,
            service=self.service,
            prompt_hash=self.prompt_hash,
            model=self.model,
            input_tokens=self.input_tokens,
            output_tokens=self.output_tokens,
            latency_ms=round(self.latency_ms, 2),
            status=self.status,
            error_type=self.error_type,
            eval_score=self.eval_score,
            **self.metadata,
        )


# Updated LLMGateway with observability
import hashlib, asyncio
from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic, APIError, RateLimitError
from llm.gateway import LLMConfig, LLMGatewayError

class ObservableLLMGateway:
    def __init__(self, client: AsyncAnthropic, config: LLMConfig, service: str):
        self._client = client
        self._config = config
        self._service = service

    async def complete(self, prompt: str, *, trace_id: str | None = None) -> str:
        event = LLMTraceEvent(
            service=self._service,
            model=self._config.model,
            prompt_hash=hashlib.sha256(prompt.encode()).hexdigest()[:16],
        )
        if trace_id:
            event.trace_id = trace_id
        start = time.monotonic()
        try:
            resp = await asyncio.wait_for(
                self._client.messages.create(
                    model=self._config.model,
                    max_tokens=self._config.max_tokens,
                    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
                ),
                timeout=self._config.timeout_seconds,
            )
            event.input_tokens = resp.usage.input_tokens
            event.output_tokens = resp.usage.output_tokens
            event.status = "ok"
            return resp.content[0].text
        except RateLimitError as e:
            event.status = "rate_limited"
            event.error_type = "RateLimitError"
            raise LLMGatewayError("Rate limited") from e
        except asyncio.TimeoutError as e:
            event.status = "timeout"
            event.error_type = "TimeoutError"
            raise LLMGatewayError("Timeout") from e
        except APIError as e:
            event.status = "error"
            event.error_type = type(e).__name__
            raise LLMGatewayError(str(e)) from e
        finally:
            event.latency_ms = (time.monotonic() - start) * 1000
            event.emit()
never log the raw prompt text in production

. Prompts often contain PII from the user context. Log a SHA256 hash of the prompt instead — this lets you detect when two requests used the same prompt without exposing the content, and you can replay a specific prompt by hash from your eval dataset.

Large GenAI integration codebases cannot be refactored all at once. The risk of a wide-scope refactor — where dozens of prompt strings are extracted and the LLM gateway is introduced in a single PR — is that a single eval regression is hard to attribute. Prefer an incremental strategy:
2. Add snapshot tests for that prompt

Each step produces a PR small enough for a thorough code review and an eval gate run narrow enough to attribute any regression directly to that step's changes. The full migration of a medium-sized codebase typically takes 4–6 weeks at this cadence — slower than a big-bang refactor but far safer.

After refactoring, the orchestrator is thin — but it still needs a test that exercises the composition of all layers to catch integration wiring errors (wrong argument order, missing trace ID propagation, incorrect error mapping).

# tests/test_query_handler.py
import pytest
from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, MagicMock
from handlers.query import QueryHandler
from llm.gateway import LLMConfig
from prompts.user_query import UserContext

@pytest.fixture
def mock_gateway():
    gw = AsyncMock()
    gw.complete.return_value = "Here is your answer."
    return gw

@pytest.fixture
def mock_user_repo():
    repo = AsyncMock()
    repo.get.return_value = UserContext(name="Alice", tier="pro", locale="en-US")
    return repo

@pytest.fixture
def handler(mock_gateway, mock_user_repo):
    return QueryHandler(gateway=mock_gateway, user_repo=mock_user_repo)

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_handler_calls_gateway_with_prompt(handler, mock_gateway, mock_user_repo):
    result = await handler.handle(query="How do I export?", user_id="u123")
    assert result == "Here is your answer."
    mock_user_repo.get.assert_called_once_with("u123")
    # Gateway was called with a non-empty prompt string
    call_args = mock_gateway.complete.call_args
    assert len(call_args.args[0]) > 50  # prompt has substance

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_handler_propagates_gateway_error(handler, mock_gateway):
    from llm.gateway import LLMGatewayError
    mock_gateway.complete.side_effect = LLMGatewayError("timeout")
    with pytest.raises(LLMGatewayError):
        await handler.handle(query="test", user_id="u456")
Refactoring GenAI integration code safely requires the same discipline as refactoring any critical system, plus one additional safeguard: an

that detects quality regressions that unit tests cannot catch. The primary structural goal is separating the prompt layer from the LLM client layer from the data layer — each becoming independently testable, reviewable by the right team, and swappable without touching the others.
The hardest habit to build is resisting the

shortcut: when a change is purely structural it is tempting to skip the eval gate. Resist — any change that touches a string flowing into a prompt deserves an eval run, however small the change appears.
